analysis.period_summary: For the week of 2026-04-06 to 2026-04-12, AstroLover Sketch generated $6,074.18 USD net revenue (ClickBank) across 115 frontend sales, with a total backend value of $6,074.18 including upsells. The single most critical funnel finding is a severely broken checkout-to-purchase rate across all active flows (5.09–6.57%), far below the 15% minimum threshold, meaning roughly 1,380 checkout clicks this week yielded only 108 tracked purchases — fixing this step is the highest-leverage opportunity in the funnel.

funnel_scorecard.commentary: The funnel's landing-to-optin and offer-to-checkout steps are performing reasonably well (17–21% optin rates and 77–86% offer CTR on main flows), but the checkout-to-purchase rate is catastrophically low across every active flow (5.09–6.57%), acting as the primary revenue ceiling. Backend monetization is average: OTO1 at 29.6% is in the average band but approaching the weak threshold, and OTO2 at 17.4% is in the average band but leaves significant revenue on the table.

funnel_leaks.why_it_matters: With ~1,380 checkout clicks this week and a 20% checkout-to-purchase rate (target floor), the funnel should be generating ~276 sales versus the ~108 tracked — recovering even half that gap at $37 AOV would add roughly $3,100+ in weekly frontend revenue before upsells.

The /b landing page's offer v2 checkout CTA rate is 60.85% versus 82–86% for /c flows, meaning approximately 86 additional checkout clicks are being left on the table each week from this flow alone, suppressing downstream purchase and upsell revenue.

The legacy /offer path shows only 4 checkout clicks from 367 offer page views (1.09% CTA rate), indicating a likely broken or missing CTA button — although sample size is too small for confidence, this path appears functionally dead and should be audited immediately.

GA4 data shows only 39.34% of visitors who reach the opt-in form page actually submit it (funnel_v4), meaning over 60% abandon at the form step — improving form conversion would directly increase the volume entering the offer page and amplify all downstream revenue.

The /c-v2 landing page converts at 14.99–15.01%, about 2–6 percentage points below the /c (17.32–17.42%) and /b (21.17%) variants, suggesting the c-v2 page copy or layout is underperforming and costing opt-in volume that feeds the rest of the funnel.

upsell_analysis.commentary: OTO1 Blueprint at 29.6% take rate is in the average band but approaching the weak threshold (<25%), and the downsell at $47 captures only 4 buyers (3.5%), indicating the price-drop offer is not compelling enough to recover decliners. OTO2 Timeline at 17.4% is average but below the 25% strong threshold — with only 21 combined OTO2 buyers, there is meaningful revenue being left at both upsell stages, and improving the OTO1 presentation could cascade positively through the entire backend sequence.

needle_movers.why: The checkout-to-purchase rate of 5.09–6.57% across all high-traffic flows is the single largest revenue bottleneck in the funnel. With 1,380 checkout clicks this week, lifting the rate from ~6% to even 15% would produce ~124 additional sales at $37 = ~$4,588 more frontend revenue weekly, plus downstream upsell uplift.
what: Audit the ClickBank checkout flow for friction: test removing or reducing upsell pre-sell bumps, check page load speed, and verify the checkout URL/hoplink is correctly configured for all flows
A/B test the checkout page headline and price presentation — consider adding a trust stack (guarantee badge, testimonials, secure payment icons) directly above the payment form
Implement checkout cart abandonment recovery via email (Maropost) triggered within 1 hour of checkout click without purchase, targeting the estimated ~1,272 non-converting clickers per week
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting checkout-to-purchase from ~6% to 15% across 1,380 weekly checkout clicks = ~124 incremental sales × $37 = ~$4,588 additional weekly frontend revenue; plus ~$1,500+ in backend upsell revenue at current OTO take rates

needle_movers.why: GA4 shows only 39.34% (funnel_v4) and 45.77% (funnel_v2) of visitors who reach the opt-in page actually submit the form. Increasing this rate from ~40% to 55% on funnel_v4 would add ~308 additional optins per week from existing traffic, feeding more buyers into the offer page.
what: Redesign the opt-in form page to reduce fields to the absolute minimum (first name + email only), ensure the CTA button copy is benefit-led (e.g. 'Reveal My Reading') rather than generic
Add social proof elements above the fold on the opt-in page (e.g. '127,000+ readings delivered') and a brief curiosity-driven headline reinforcing what the subscriber will receive
Test a progress-indicator or 'step 1 of 2' framing on the opt-in page to signal low commitment and increase form completions
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Adding ~308 incremental weekly optins (funnel_v4 at 39%→55%) × 83% offer CTA rate × current ~6.5% checkout conversion × $37 = ~$62 immediate frontend lift; primary value is in compounding volume for checkout fix — at 15% checkout rate, same increment = ~$141 weekly frontend + upsell tail

needle_movers.why: The /b flow's offer-to-checkout rate of 60.85% is 20+ percentage points below the /c flows (82–86%), representing approximately 86 suppressed checkout clicks per week from this flow alone. Closing this gap would meaningfully increase purchase volume.
what: Compare the /offer/v2 sales page CTA button placement, copy, and visibility against /offer/v4 — specifically check if the CTA appears above the fold or requires excessive scrolling

Review whether the /b landing page audience or traffic source creates a different intent profile that the v2 offer page copy does not match — consider testing /b with /offer/v4 to isolate the page effect
Add a sticky CTA bar or mid-page CTA insertion on /offer/v2 to capture users who engage with the content but do not scroll to the bottom CTA
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting /b → /offer/v2 offer-to-checkout rate from 60.85% to 80% = ~69 additional weekly checkout clicks; at 15% checkout conversion (post-fix) × $37 = ~$383 incremental weekly frontend revenue from this flow

needle_movers.why: OTO1 at 29.6% is in the average band but only 4 percentage points above the 'weak' threshold. At 115 frontend sales and $67 price, every 10 percentage point improvement in OTO1 take rate generates ~$770 in additional weekly revenue — and the downsell at 3.5% suggests the current decline-to-downsell funnel is nearly inert.
what: Rewrite the OTO1 Blueprint sales page with a stronger immediate-value hook — lead with a specific, tangible outcome (e.g. '34-page done-for-you blueprint that doubles your reading accuracy in 7 days') rather than a generic upsell pitch
Add a video VSL (even 2–3 minutes) to the OTO1 page — visual demonstrations of the Blueprint content can significantly lift take rates for digital info products

Redesign the OTO1 downsell at $47 with a more compelling scarcity or exclusivity angle to recover more decliners; the current 3.5% downsell take rate indicates the offer or messaging is failing to convert fence-sitters
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting OTO1 take rate from 29.6% to 40% on 115 weekly buyers = ~12 incremental OTO1 sales × $67 = ~$804 additional weekly backend revenue

needle_movers.area: Postback tracking gap — CPV vs ClickBank reconciliation
needle_movers.why: CPV Labs records 108 conversions versus 115 ClickBank frontend sales (7 missing, ~6% gap), and the revenue difference is $304.49 USD (5%). While within a caution threshold, untracked conversions mean campaign optimization decisions are being made on incomplete data, potentially causing under-scaling of profitable campaigns or misattribution.
what: Audit the ClickBank IPN/postback configuration for all active campaigns — verify the postback URL is correctly firing for every SKU and that no ClickBank order flow bypasses the CPV Labs tracking pixel
Cross-reference the 7 missing sales against campaign 105 (highest volume, 74 CPV conversions vs likely 80+ CB sales) to identify if the postback gap is concentrated in one campaign
Set up a weekly reconciliation alert in CPV Labs that flags any week where CPV conversions fall more than 3% below ClickBank frontend sales count
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Directional: accurate tracking enables correct bid optimization and budget allocation across campaigns, preventing spend waste on under-attributed campaigns and enabling confident scaling of campaign 105 which appears to be the primary revenue driver

cpv_cb_discrepancy.note: CPV Labs records 108 conversions vs 115 ClickBank frontend sales — a 7-sale gap (~6%) suggesting incomplete postback firing; revenue difference of $304.49 USD (5%) is within caution range but warrants postback audit to ensure campaign optimization data is accurate.

analysis.data_notes: The 'landing→offer' flow shows a severe anomaly: 25 landing page views but 367 offer page views, meaning offer_page_views is ~14.7× larger than optin_completions (1), which violates the expected optin_completions ≈ offer_page_views relationship — this suggests the /offer path is receiving direct traffic or has a broken tracking setup rather than being fed exclusively through the opt-in funnel, and its metrics (particularly the 1.09% offer-to-checkout rate and 25% checkout-to-purchase from only 4 clicks) should not be used for optimization decisions. The 'landing→offer_v3' and 'a→offer_v2' flows each have fewer than 20 checkout clicks (7 and 1 respectively) — findings from these flows are directional only and not statistically reliable. GA4 supplement data covers funnel_v4 and funnel_v2 only; flows through /c/, /c-v2/, and /b/ landing pages are partially mapped to these GA4 funnels but exact flow-level GA4 attribution is not available. Campaign 100 shows 12.5% CR from only 8 views — this is a statistical outlier and should not be treated as a meaningful performance signal.
